data.title: Wilmington Trust’s CIO says market downturn probably isn’t done
data.description:
Wilmington Trust Investment Advisors chief investment officer Tony Roth warned of mounting economic risks from U.S.-China trade tensions, urging the Trump administration to show progress in negotiations by Memorial Day to avoid recessionary pressures.

During a CNBC interview, Roth emphasized that current tariffs represent a 2.7% GDP tax hike – the largest since the 1970s – with small businesses facing existential supply chain threats.

“The complexity of this relationship is so great – we don’t expect an agreement for quite some time,” he said.

Roth noted the market’s dwindling patience, citing JPMorgan analysts’ skepticism about achieving “90 deals in 90 days” to ease trade friction. He argued for interim tariff reductions to prevent cascading price increases across vulnerable industries.

Also, while acknowledging the Federal Reserve’s stance that tariffs aren’t structurally inflationary, Roth warned consumer sensitivity to price hikes could create “upward spirals” in sectors reliant on Chinese imports.

The CIO dismissed Fitch Ratings’ prediction of delayed rate cuts until Q4, stating, that “we see potential rate cuts probably in June” with four reductions anticipated in 2025 to counter slowing growth.

He criticized Washington’s “cause-and-effect” policy approach, stressing that small businesses “are not on the administration’s surface” despite bearing the brunt of supply chain disruptions.

With tariffs at “prohibitive” levels, Roth cautioned that failure to recalibrate trade strategy risks pushing the economy into recession as early as this summer. His comments underscore deepening market concerns that political timelines and economic realities are misaligned, urging policymakers to accelerate diplomatic engagement before tariff impacts become irreversible.

Roth’s outlook suggests investors should brace for both heightened volatility and potential Fed intervention as trade tensions reshape growth forecasts. He emphasized that the failure to show meaningful progress in U.S.-China trade relations could have severe consequences for economic stability and market performance in the coming months.

data.title: Palantir leads enterprise software stocks down as Powell warns over tariffs
data.description:
Enterprise software stocks — and the broader market in general — took another leg lower on Wednesday after Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell warned over the lasting impact that tariffs could have on inflation.

“As we gain a better understanding of the policy changes, we will have a better sense of the implications for the economy, and hence for monetary policy,” Powell said in his prepared remarks to the Economic Club of Chicago. “Tariffs are highly likely to generate at least a temporary rise in inflation. The inflationary effects could also be more persistent.”

Following the remarks, Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) fell more than 6.5%, while Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), Adobe (ADBE) and Oracle (ORCL) dipped more than 2.5%.

Cybersecurity stocks, which some have said could be seen as “defensive” amid the recent volatility, also fell on Wednesday. Fortinet (FTNT) fell 2.7%, while CrowdStrike (CRWD) lost 1.4%. Palo Alto Networks (PANW) declined 1.6%, CyberArk (CYBR) fell 1%, and SentinelOne (S) lost 2%.

data.title: United airlines projects $7-$9 EPS in potential recession scenario for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United Airlines (UAL) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott Kirby emphasized United's resilience in the face of a weaker macroeconomic environment, citing its ability to attract and retain brand-loyal customers. He highlighted that the company achieved its highest first-quarter pretax margin since the pandemic and expects to remain profitable even in challenging conditions.
  • Kirby projected full-year earnings per share (EPS) of $7 to $9 in a potential recession scenario, underscoring financial resilience and structural changes in the business.
  • President Brett Hart reported operational improvements, including achieving the highest on-time departure rate among U.S. large peers and record net promoter scores (NPS). The company also noted safety enhancements and pilot training investments.
  • CFO Mike Leskinen stated that Q1 EPS reached $0.91, supported by effective cost management and lower fuel expenses, within the guidance range. He confirmed a robust liquidity position of $18.3 billion and $2 billion in free cash flow generation, with $1 billion of debt repaid during the quarter.

Outlook

  • Management maintained full-year 2025 EPS guidance of $11.50 to $13.50, contingent on current booking trends stabilizing. Leskinen noted the absence of contingency in this guidance but expressed confidence in achieving the targets if trends hold.
  • Capacity adjustments include a planned 4% reduction in domestic capacity starting in Q3 2025 to address weaker demand, particularly during off-peak periods.
  • The company remains optimistic about international markets, noting strong demand in the Pacific and Atlantic regions.

Financial Results

  • Q1 2025 revenue increased 5.4% year-over-year to $13.2 billion, setting a company record for the quarter. Premium cabin unit revenues performed well, with mid-single-digit growth.
  • Domestic main cabin revenues declined due to weaker demand, particularly during off-peak hours, leading to adjustments in capacity.
  • Loyalty program revenues grew 9% to $1.5 billion, reflecting strong co-branded credit card spending.
  • United reported a pretax margin of 3% for Q1 2025, a year-over-year improvement of 3.6 percentage points.

Q&A

  • Analyst Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: Questioned the impact of a potential recession on 2026 earnings forecasts. CEO Kirby indicated that margins would likely be higher post-recession, supported by structural changes and a focus on brand loyalty.
  • Analyst Andrew Didora, Bank of America: Inquired about cost performance and recession scenarios. CFO Leskinen explained that Q1 cost management included specific measures such as early aircraft retirements to reduce maintenance expenses, and recession scenarios assume further capacity adjustments.
  • Analyst Conor Cunningham, Melius Research: Asked about capacity plans amid domestic demand weakness. Management highlighted tactical reductions in off-peak flights and a focus on maintaining margins while building network strength.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a mix of skepticism and curiosity regarding the company's ability to sustain guidance amid macroeconomic uncertainties. Questions often sought clarity on cost controls, capacity adjustments, and recession planning.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing structural improvements, financial resilience, and strategic investments.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained cautiously optimistic, with increased emphasis on managing through potential economic challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for the full year remained unchanged from Q4 2024, but management acknowledged reduced contingency in achieving targets.
  • Domestic capacity adjustments were more pronounced in Q1 2025, reflecting a sharper focus on reducing unprofitable flying in response to weaker demand.
  • Analysts' focus shifted slightly from international growth opportunities in Q4 2024 to domestic demand challenges and recession preparedness in Q1 2025.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted potential risks from economic uncertainty, including a possible recession and its impact on bookings.
  • Tariff-related risks to aircraft procurement were mentioned, though United's reliance on domestic production for Airbus deliveries mitigates this concern.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of premium demand and the impact of reduced domestic utilization on margins.

Final Takeaway

United Airlines demonstrated resilience in Q1 2025 despite a weaker macroeconomic environment, achieving record revenue and maintaining profitability. Management outlined plans to adjust capacity and leverage brand loyalty to navigate potential economic challenges, projecting EPS of $11.50 to $13.50 for the year and $7 to $9 in a recession scenario. With strong liquidity and a focus on premium services, United aims to sustain its competitive advantage in a challenging market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tariffs could create tension between Fed's dual mandate goals, Fed Chair Jerome Powell says
data.description:
The level of tariffs announced so far have been "significantly larger" than the Federal Reserve anticipated, making it "likely" that the same will be true of its economic effects, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Wednesday.

That could result in higher inflation and slower growth, he said in the prepared text for a speech at the Economic Club of Chicago. Tariffs are "highly likely to generate at least a temporary rise in inflation. The inflationary effects could also be more persistent," he said.

The Federal Reserve could find itself in a scenario where its dual-mandate goals of maximum employment and price stability "are in tension... If that were to occur, we would consider how far the economy is from each goal, and the potentially different time horizons over which those respective gaps would be anticipated to close,"

As for monetary policy, "we are well positioned to wait for greater clarity before considering any adjustments to our policy stance," Powell said.

The prepared speech will be followed by moderated Q&A.

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: BlackRock eyes opportunity as tariff pause signals softer U.S. trade stance
data.description:
The U.S. administration’s recent 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs and exemption of key technology imports is being viewed as a sign of growing economic pragmatism BlackRock believes. The move suggests Washington is weighing not only geopolitical strategy but also the financial risks, inflationary pressures, and global willingness to cooperate.

While U.S.-China trade tensions continue to simmer, the temporary easing signals that there are limits to the administration’s previously hardline stance. In response, BlackRock, one of the world’s largest asset managers, adjusted its outlook, extending its tactical investment horizon from three to six–12 months. 

“Checks on policy allowed us to extend our tactical horizon back to six to 12 months and resume our positive view on U.S. and Japanese stocks,” BlackRock said in its latest weekly market commentary report. 

As a result, the investment institution sees the pause as an opportunity to dial up risk amid signs that the administration may be open to a more balanced trade approach.

Still, BlackRock warns that tariffs remain a drag on global growth and a potential driver of inflation. Uncertainty around long-term policy continues to cloud the outlook, keeping investors on alert despite near-term optimism.

S&P 500 Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS) and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

China ETFs: (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:FLCH), (BATS:CNYA), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), and (NYSEARCA:YINN).

data.title: SA analyst downgrades: AMZN, PLTR, CAT, SNOW, BKNG, ABR, RBRK
data.description:
Here are the latest rating downgrades issued by Seeking Alpha analysts:

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN). Buy to hold by Simple Investment Ideas. "Given the growing Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Gemini threat, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) should be downgraded to a hold at its current market capitalization of $1.96T and relatively high P/E of 33. While Amazon (AMZN) is still relatively well-positioned in the AI landscape, the rapid rise of Gemini could throw a major wrench in Amazon's (AMZN) long-term plans."

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR). Buy to hold by Dilantha De Silva. "Given the expensive valuation, I am not comfortable investing new money in (NASDAQ:PLTR) PLTR today. However, given my understanding of the strength of the company's moat, I feel comfortable holding the shares I own unless I see its competitive advantages beginning to weaken."

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT). Hold to sell by Sungarden Investment."CAT (NYSE:CAT) stock is sell rated because it was so overvalued at $400 a share; $300 a share still does not bring it into range for us in the current environment."

Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW). Hold to sell by Gary Alexander. "With expensive valuation multiples, slowing growth and net revenue retention rates subject to a pullback in customer spending, and rapid hiring that is preventing margin progression, Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW) is looking very shaky in 2025 amid any tariff-related macro fallouts."

Booking (NASDAQ:BKNG). Buy to hold by Florian Muller. "I maintain a long-term bullish stance on BKNG (NASDAQ:BKNG) stock, but shifted to a temporary Hold, recognizing potential geopolitical risks."

Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR). Buy to hold by Joseph Parrish. "Arbor Realty Trust's (NYSE:ABR) downgrade to Hold is due to realized loan book weaknesses and a slower growth outlook, with shares down 30% in six months."

Rubrik (NYSE:RBRK). Buy to hold by Gary Alexander. "While I remain convinced that Rubrik (NYSE:RBRK) is a strong company in the long term, I think sentiment will continue to shift against this stock as growth is expected to slow in the first half of FY26, due to macro pressures plus Rubrik's (RBRK) sales incentive plan change."

data.title: China Beige Book CEO calls for clarity in U.S. negotiation strategy amid trade tensions
data.description:
Amid escalating U.S.-China trade tensions, China Beige Book CEO Leland Miller emphasized the need for clarity in Washington’s negotiation strategy.

During a CNBC interview, Miller noted that while both sides could resume talks by managing optics, President Donald Trump's administration has yet to articulate concrete objectives.

“They’ve articulated every kind of option back and forth and haven’t been very clear on what they actually want,” he said, highlighting how this ambiguity complicates efforts to secure meaningful concessions from Beijing.

Miller observed that the current tariff plan with China creates leverage but warned economic pressures could push Washington to seek “goodies on the side” like fentanyl cooperation or TikTok restrictions without a coherent plan.

“The hype has not yet articulated what we actually want the outcome of talks to look like,” he stated, underscoring the risks of piecemeal negotiations.

China retains strategic advantages, including dominance in critical mineral supply chains. Miller highlighted Beijing’s gradual weaponization of these resources: “They already are signaling that, from the supply chain standpoint, they do have leverage.”

However, he cautioned that drastic financial moves like dumping U.S. Treasuries remain unlikely due to China’s entrenched reliance on dollar-denominated systems.

Global partners present another challenge. While China seeks to exploit U.S. tariff disputes with allies, most nations remain wary of overreliance, Miller said.

“Southeast Asian countries will have to talk to China, but I don’t think they want to get in bed with China,” he said. European pushback against Chinese overcapacity exports further limits Beijing’s ability to isolate Washington.

Ultimately, Miller stressed that successful negotiations require the U.S. to define clear priorities – whether reducing trade deficits, securing tech safeguards, or countering economic coercion.

Without this focus, he warned, talks risk becoming a “messaging issue” rather than a path to resolving the world’s most consequential trade rift.

data.title: U.S. Army Corps to fast-track review of Enbridge's Line 5 tunnel under emergency status
data.description:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers said Wednesday it will expedite its permitting process for Enbridge's (NYSE:ENB) Line 5 tunnel under the Straits of Mackinac after determining the pipeline project meets national energy emergency status declared by President Trump.

Trump had ordered the Army Corps to issue permits enabling the filling of wetlands and dredging or building in waterways as part of his January 20 emergency energy declaration, which was aimed at expanding U.S. energy production.

In its public notice, the Army Corps' Detroit district said the emergency permit request met the terms of Trump's executive order because it addresses "an energy supply situation which would result in an unacceptable hazard to life, a significant loss of property, or an immediate, unforeseen, and significant economic hardship" if not acted upon more quickly.

"We're not eliminating steps," Shane McCoy, head of regulatory affairs for the Army Corps' Detroit office, told The Detroit News. "The emergency procedures, as outlined in regulation, just truncate the timeline, [which] will result in a very legally defensible and very well-informed decision in our record of decision."

Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) has been seeking approvals for its $750M tunnel project under the Great Lakes to house its aging Line 5 oil pipeline for nearly five years; Michigan regulators had approved its application to build the project in 2023, but the company had awaited Army Corps permitting.
